plan_title: FlowOS — Distributed Human + Machine Orchestration Platform: Full Implementation Plan

phase_1.title: Project Foundation — Monorepo, Config, Shared Infrastructure
phase_1.output_preview:
## Phase Complete: Project Foundation — Monorepo, Config, Shared Infrastructure

All 12 tasks have been completed with production-quality deliverables. Here's what was built:

### Task 1 — `flowos/pyproject.toml`
Updated the existing minimal pyproject.toml with the full dependency set: `temporalio`, `confluent-kafka`, `click`, `fastapi`, `uvicorn`, `sqlalchemy`, `alembic`, `gitpython`, `pydantic`, `pydantic-settings`, `langgraph`, `langchain`, `langchain-community`, plus supporting libraries (asyncpg, boto3, structlog, pyyaml, rich, tenacity). Dev extras include `pytest`, `pytest-asyncio`, `ruff`, `mypy`.

### Task 2 — `flowos/Makefile`
Comprehensive Makefile with all required targets:
- `make up` — starts all Docker services with health-wait
- `make down` — stops services cleanly
- `make migrate` — runs Alembic `upgrade head`
- `make test` — runs pytest with coverage
- `make lint` — runs ruff linter
Plus bonus targets: `make dev` (full bootstrap), `make kafka-topics`, `make format`, `make typecheck`, `make logs`, `make ps`, `make clean`, and more.

### Task 3 — `flowos/docker-compose.yml`
Full service stack with health checks:
- **Zookeeper** (confluentinc/cp-zookeeper:7.7.1) — Kafka coordination
- **Kafka** (confluentinc/cp-kafka:7.7.1) — Central event bus, dual listeners (internal/external)
- **Kafka UI** (provectuslabs/kafka-ui) — Web management interface on port 8080
- **PostgreSQL 16** — Metadata store with init.sql mount
- **Temporal** (temporalio/auto-setup:1.25.2) — Durable workflow orchestration on port 7233
- **Temporal UI** (temporalio/ui:2.32.0) — Workflow visualization on port 8088
- **MinIO** — S3-compatible object storage with console on port 9001
- **MinIO Init** — Auto-creates required buckets (artifacts, snapshots, checkpoints, logs)

### Task 4 — `flowos/docker-compose.override.yml`
Local dev overrides with hot-reload volume mounts for API and orchestrator services, debug ports (debugpy), verbose logging, and environment variable injection for al

phase_2.title: Domain Models & Database Schema
phase_2.output_preview:
## Domain Models & Database Schema — Complete

### What Was Accomplished

**8 Pydantic Domain Models (shared/models/):**

1. **`shared/models/event.py`** — `EventRecord` with full Kafka event envelope: `EventType` (40+ event types), `EventTopic` (8 topics), `EventSource`, `EventSeverity`. Includes topic/event_type alignment validation, Kafka serialization helpers, and immutable frozen model.

2. **`shared/models/workflow.py`** — `Workflow` + `WorkflowDefinition` + `WorkflowStep`. Covers full lifecycle (PENDING→RUNNING→COMPLETED/FAILED/CANCELLED/PAUSED), `WorkflowStatus`, `WorkflowTrigger` enums, step dependency validation, duration/active/terminal properties.

3. **`shared/models/task.py`** — `Task` + `TaskInput` + `TaskOutput` + `TaskAttempt`. Full lifecycle (PENDING→ASSIGNED→ACCEPTED→IN_PROGRESS→CHECKPOINTED/HANDOFF/REVERT/COMPLETED/FAILED), `TaskStatus`, `TaskPriority`, `TaskType` enums, retry bounds validation.

4. **`shared/models/agent.py`** — `Agent` + `AgentCapability` + `AgentHeartbeat`. Covers human/AI/build/deploy/system agent types, capability declaration, heartbeat records, availability/online properties.

5. **`shared/models/workspace.py`** — `Workspace` + `GitState` + `WorkspaceSnapshot`. Git-backed workspace with branch/commit tracking, dirty state, snapshot history, disk usage limits, task branch naming.

6. **`shared/models/checkpoint.py`** — `Checkpoint` + `CheckpointFile`. Durable task state snapshots with Git commit SHA, file-level change tracking, progress percentage, S3 archive key, sequence numbers.

7. **`shared/models/handoff.py`** — `Handoff` + `HandoffContext`. Task ownership transfers with briefing context, rejection/failure reasons, expiry, response time tracking, source/target validation.

8. **`shared/models/reasoning.py`** — `ReasoningSession` + `ReasoningStep` + `ToolCall`. Complete AI reasoning trace with LangGraph/LangSmith integration, token usage, cost estimation, human review flags.

**SQLAlchemy ORM Models (shared/db/models.py

phase_5.title: Temporal Orchestrator — Workflows, Activities & DSL Parser
phase_5.output_preview:
## Temporal Orchestrator — Workflows, Activities & DSL Parser

All 12 tasks completed successfully. Created 15 production-ready Python files totaling ~5,089 lines of code.

### Files Created

**Worker:**
- `flowos/orchestrator/worker.py` — Temporal worker entry point with graceful shutdown, TLS support, Kafka topic initialization, health check server, and full workflow/activity registration

**Workflows:**
- `flowos/orchestrator/workflows/__init__.py` — Package exports
- `flowos/orchestrator/workflows/base_workflow.py` — Abstract base class with signal handlers (pause/resume/cancel), query handlers (get_status/get_step_result), shared activity helpers, retry policies, and workflow lifecycle management
- `flowos/orchestrator/workflows/feature_delivery.py` — End-to-end feature delivery workflow (plan → implement → build → review → optional approval) with workspace provisioning, step tracking, and cancellation handling
- `flowos/orchestrator/workflows/build_and_review.py` — CI/CD build-and-review workflow with quality gate evaluation, parallel step support, and AI analysis integration

**Activities:**
- `flowos/orchestrator/activities/__init__.py` — Package exports
- `flowos/orchestrator/activities/task_activities.py` — create_task, assign_task, wait_for_task_completion (with heartbeats), update_task_status, get_task_status, cancel_task
- `flowos/orchestrator/activities/checkpoint_activities.py` — create_checkpoint_record, verify_checkpoint, revert_to_checkpoint, list_task_checkpoints
- `flowos/orchestrator/activities/handoff_activities.py` — initiate_handoff, wait_for_handoff_acceptance (with heartbeats), complete_handoff, cancel_handoff
- `flowos/orchestrator/activities/workspace_activities.py` — provision_workspace, sync_workspace, archive_workspace, get_workspace_status
- `flowos/orchestrator/activities/kafka_activities.py` — publish_workflow_event, publish_task_event, publish_workspace_event

**DSL:**
- `flowos/orchestrator/dsl/__init__.py` — Package exports

phase_8.title: AI Worker & LangGraph/LangChain Reasoning Layer
phase_8.output_preview:
## AI Worker & LangGraph/LangChain Reasoning Layer — Complete

Successfully implemented all 10 tasks for the FlowOS AI Worker and LangGraph/LangChain Reasoning Layer. All 12 files pass syntax validation and export verification.

### Files Created (5,614 lines total)

#### Core Worker
- **`flowos/workers/ai_worker.py`** (968 lines) — The main AI Worker that:
  - Subscribes to `flowos.task.events` as consumer group `flowos-ai-agent`
  - Handles `TASK_ASSIGNED` events for AI task types (ai, review, analysis)
  - Selects the appropriate LangGraph graph based on task tags/name
  - Runs graphs in background threads (configurable concurrency limit)
  - Emits `TASK_ACCEPTED`, `AI_TASK_STARTED`, `AI_REASONING_TRACE`, `AI_SUGGESTION_CREATED`, `TASK_COMPLETED`/`TASK_FAILED` events
  - Writes full reasoning trace to `.flowos/reasoning_trace.json`
  - Supports OpenAI, Anthropic, and FakeListChatModel (for testing)
  - Graceful shutdown with signal handling

#### Context Loader
- **`flowos/ai/context_loader.py`** (685 lines) — Assembles complete task context:
  - Reads workspace metadata from `.flowos/workspace.json` and `.flowos/task.json`
  - Loads Git state (branch, commits, changed files with diffs) via GitPython
  - Scans `artifacts/` directory for build logs, test results, patches
  - Loads prior reasoning sessions from `.flowos/reasoning_trace.json`
  - Infers build/test status from artifact content
  - Renders context as structured prompt text for LLM consumption
  - `TaskContext` dataclass with full serialisation support

#### LangGraph Reasoning Graphs
- **`flowos/ai/graphs/code_review.py`** (712 lines) — Multi-step code review graph:
  - Nodes: load_context → analyse_changes → security_check → quality_check → synthesise → emit_suggestion
  - Produces structured findings with severity ratings (critical/high/medium/low)
  - Emits `AI_SUGGESTION_CREATED` and `AI_REVIEW_COMPLETED` Kafka events
  - Returns outcome: approved/needs_changes/rejected

- **`flowos/ai/graphs/fix_

phase_9.title: Policy Engine
phase_9.output_preview:
## Policy Engine Phase — Complete

### What Was Built

A comprehensive, production-ready Policy Engine for FlowOS consisting of 7 Python files:

#### Core Architecture

**`policy/evaluator.py`** — Core evaluation primitives:
- `EvaluationOutcome` enum: ALLOW, DENY, PENDING_APPROVAL, SKIP
- `EvaluationContext` — immutable frozen dataclass carrying event + agent metadata (type, roles, tags, branch info, handoff type)
- `EvaluationResult` — structured per-rule result with severity, remediation hints, approvers
- `AggregateEvaluationResult` — aggregated result with DENY > PENDING_APPROVAL > ALLOW precedence
- `PolicyRule` — abstract base class with `applies_to()` / `evaluate()` interface + convenience factories (`_allow`, `_deny`, `_pending_approval`, `_skip`)
- `PolicyEvaluator` — orchestrates rules in priority order, fail-fast mode, exception-safe evaluation

**`policy/engine.py`** — Central coordinator:
- Consumes from `flowos.workflow.events`, `flowos.task.events`, `flowos.workspace.events`, `flowos.build.events`
- Enriches events into `EvaluationContext` (extracts agent type from payload/source, branch names, handoff types, RBAC roles)
- Evaluates via `PolicyEvaluator`
- Emits `POLICY_EVALUATED` (always), `POLICY_VIOLATION_DETECTED` (DENY), `APPROVAL_REQUESTED` (PENDING_APPROVAL), `BRANCH_PROTECTION_TRIGGERED` (branch rule fires)
- Long-lived consumer loop with SIGINT/SIGTERM graceful shutdown
- Loads 4 default rules on startup: MachineSafety (p=5), BranchProtection (p=10), AI Handoff Gate (p=15), AI Patch Gate (p=25)

#### Policy Rules

**`policy/rules/branch_protection.py`** — Git branch protection:
- Configurable protected branch patterns (glob: `main`, `production`, `release/*`, etc.)
- AI agents always DENIED on protected branches (configurable)
- BUILD/DEPLOY agents allowed (configurable)
- SYSTEM agents always allowed
- RBAC role check (maintainer, release-manager, admin)
- Falls back to PENDING_APPROVAL when `require_approval=True`

**`policy/rules/approval

phase_10.title: REST API & WebSocket Server
phase_10.output_preview:
## REST API & WebSocket Server — Complete

Built a production-ready FastAPI REST API and WebSocket server for FlowOS with 49 routes across 6 resource domains.

### Files Created

#### Core Application
- **`flowos/api/__init__.py`** — Package init, exports `create_app`
- **`flowos/api/main.py`** — FastAPI application factory with lifespan management (Kafka producer, Temporal client, WebSocket bridge), CORS middleware, request logging, health/readiness endpoints, and WebSocket route at `/ws`
- **`flowos/api/dependencies.py`** — FastAPI dependency injection: async DB session, Kafka producer, Temporal client, agent ID extraction from `X-Agent-ID` header, pagination params, request ID tracking

#### WebSocket Server
- **`flowos/api/ws_server.py`** — Full WebSocket infrastructure:
  - `BroadcastManager` — thread-safe per-client asyncio.Queue management
  - `KafkaBroadcastBridge` — bridges sync Kafka consumer (ConsumerGroup.UI_STREAM) to async broadcast
  - `WebSocketManager` — handles WebSocket lifecycle with filtering and heartbeats
  - `websocket_endpoint` — FastAPI WebSocket handler

#### REST Routers
- **`flowos/api/routers/__init__.py`** — Package init, exports all routers
- **`flowos/api/routers/workflows.py`** — 8 routes: POST/GET/PATCH workflows, cancel/pause/resume lifecycle, list tasks per workflow. Publishes WORKFLOW_CREATED/CANCELLED/PAUSED/RESUMED Kafka events
- **`flowos/api/routers/tasks.py`** — 9 routes: GET/PATCH tasks, accept/start/complete/fail/cancel lifecycle, list attempts. Publishes TASK_ACCEPTED/STARTED/COMPLETED/FAILED/UPDATED events
- **`flowos/api/routers/agents.py`** — 10 routes: register/list/get/update agents, heartbeat, deregister, list tasks/capabilities, upsert/delete capabilities. Publishes AGENT_REGISTERED/ONLINE/OFFLINE/BUSY/IDLE/CAPABILITY_UPDATED events
- **`flowos/api/routers/checkpoints.py`** — 4 routes: list/get checkpoints, list files, revert. Publishes CHECKPOINT_REVERTED event
- **`flowos/api/routers/handoffs.py`** — 7 routes: c

phase_11.title: Observability Consumer & Metrics
phase_11.output_preview:
## Observability Consumer & Metrics — Complete

All four tasks have been implemented and verified:

### Files Created

#### 1. `flowos/observability/metrics.py` (593 lines)
Defines the complete Prometheus metrics registry for FlowOS:
- **Event bus metrics**: `flowos_events_consumed_total`, `flowos_events_processing_errors_total`, `flowos_event_processing_latency_seconds`, `flowos_consumer_lag`
- **Workflow metrics**: `flowos_workflow_events_total`, `flowos_workflows_active` (gauge), `flowos_workflow_duration_seconds` (histogram), `flowos_workflows_completed_total`
- **Task metrics**: `flowos_task_events_total`, `flowos_tasks_active` (gauge), `flowos_task_duration_seconds`, `flowos_task_handoffs_total`, `flowos_task_checkpoints_total`, `flowos_task_reverts_total`
- **Agent metrics**: `flowos_agent_events_total`, `flowos_agents_by_status` (gauge), `flowos_agents_registered_total`
- **Build/test metrics**: `flowos_build_events_total`, `flowos_build_outcomes_total`, `flowos_test_outcomes_total`, `flowos_artifacts_uploaded_total`
- **AI reasoning metrics**: `flowos_ai_events_total`, `flowos_ai_suggestions_total`, `flowos_ai_patches_total`, `flowos_ai_tool_calls_total`, `flowos_ai_reasoning_traces_total`, `flowos_ai_tokens_used` (histogram)
- **Workspace/Git metrics**: `flowos_workspace_events_total`, `flowos_checkpoints_created_total`, `flowos_checkpoints_reverted_total`, `flowos_branches_created_total`
- **Policy metrics**: `flowos_policy_events_total`, `flowos_policy_violations_total`, `flowos_approval_events_total`
- **Observability/system metrics**: `flowos_observability_events_total`, `flowos_system_alerts_total`, `flowos_health_check_results_total`, `flowos_last_event_processed_timestamp_seconds`, `flowos_audit_log_writes_total`, `flowos_audit_log_write_latency_seconds`
- **`create_metrics_app()`**: Returns a FastAPI app with `/metrics` (Prometheus text format), `/health`, and `/` endpoints

phase_12.title: React UI — Workflow Graph & Live Event Feed
phase_12.output_preview:
## React UI — Workflow Graph & Live Event Feed: Complete

All 11 tasks have been completed successfully. The FlowOS React UI is fully implemented, TypeScript-clean, and builds successfully.

### What was built:

**1. React Project Initialization**
- Initialized with `npm create vite@latest ui -- --template react-ts`
- Downgraded to Vite 5 + React 18 for Node.js 20.18 compatibility
- Production build succeeds: 430KB JS bundle, 23KB CSS

**2. Dependencies Installed**
- `@xyflow/react` v12.10.2 — workflow DAG visualization
- `zustand` v5 — global state management
- `axios` v1.7 — REST API client
- `date-fns` v3.6 — timestamp formatting
- `lucide-react` v0.460 — icon library
- `tailwindcss` v3.4 + postcss + autoprefixer — utility CSS

**3. `ui/src/store/workflowStore.ts`**
- Zustand store with `subscribeWithSelector` middleware
- Full domain types: `Workflow`, `Task`, `FlowEvent`, `WsStatus`
- Actions: setWorkflows, selectWorkflow, upsertWorkflow, upsertTask, pushEvent, setWsStatus, selectTask, setEventFilter, setTaskFilter
- Derived selectors: selectFilteredTasks, selectFilteredEvents, selectWorkflowEvents, selectSelectedTask
- Ring buffer for events (max 500, newest-first)

**4. `ui/src/api/client.ts`**
- Axios instance with `/api` base URL (Vite proxy → localhost:8000)
- Full typed endpoints: listWorkflows, getWorkflow, createWorkflow, cancelWorkflow, pauseWorkflow, resumeWorkflow, listTasks, getTask, listWorkflowTasks, listEvents, listWorkflowEvents, listAgents, healthCheck
- Error normalisation with `normaliseError()`

**5. `ui/src/hooks/useWorkflowSocket.ts`**
- Persistent WebSocket to `/ws` endpoint
- Exponential back-off reconnect (1s → 30s max)
- Heartbeat ping detection
- Optional workflow_id / event_type filter query params
- Dispatches events to Zustand store via pushEvent
- Clean teardown on unmount

**6. `ui/src/hooks/useWorkflowGraph.ts`**
- Derives React Flow nodes + edges from Zustand task state
- Topological sort DAG layout (left-to-right columns)
- Po

phase_16.title: Verification & Smoke Test
phase_16.output_preview:
## Verification & Smoke Test — Complete

### What Was Done

**Step 1 — Project type identified:** Full-stack Python monorepo (FastAPI + Temporal + Kafka + React SPA). Tests run with pytest; UI builds with Vite/TypeScript.

**Step 2 — Module imports validated:** All core modules (`api`, `shared.models.*`, `shared.config`, `orchestrator.dsl.parser`, `workers.*`, `policy.*`) import cleanly under Python 3.12.7.

**Step 3 — Dependencies:** All Python deps pre-installed in Anaconda env (pytest 9.0.2, pydantic, fastapi, gitpython, etc.). UI deps installed via `npm install`.

**Step 4 — UI Build:** `npm run build` in `ui/` passes — 2106 modules transformed, `dist/index.html` + JS/CSS assets produced successfully.

**Step 5 — Server startup:** Skipped (requires live Docker Compose stack: Postgres, Kafka, Temporal, MinIO). API module imports cleanly; startup blocked only by missing infrastructure.

**Step 6 — Tests: 428 passed, 0 failed.**

### Bugs Fixed

1. **`tests/unit/test_dsl_parser.py`** — `TestParseExamplePipeline` expected 4 steps / `"Plan Feature"` / dep `"plan"` but the actual `feature_delivery_pipeline.yaml` has 6 steps (design→implement→ai-review→build→review→publish). Fixed: `len == 6`, `"Design Feature"`, dep `"design"`.

2. **`tests/test_artifact_uploader.py`** — `test_build_url_minio` used `patch.object(type(dict), "__getitem__", ...)` which raises `TypeError: cannot set '__getitem__' attribute of immutable type 'dict'`. Removed the invalid outer `patch.object`; the inner `patch("workers.artifact_uploader.settings")` is sufficient.

3. **`tests/integration/test_workflow_lifecycle.py`** — Two classes: file-based tests (`TestWorkflowFromExampleFile`) correctly updated to `== 6`; inline-YAML tests (`TestWorkflowCreation`, `TestWorkflowDefinitionParsing`) use a 4-step constant and were incorrectly changed to `== 6` during bulk replace — reverted to `== 4`.

4. **`flowos/.env`** — Created from `.env.example` (was missing; Python config loader reads it at import ti

waves.reason: Planning must complete before any execution phases begin
Identical deps: phase_1, phase_10, phase_11, phase_2, phase_3, phase_4, phase_5, phase_6, phase_7, phase_8, phase_9
Verification runs after all implementation phases are complete

dependencies.reason: Domain models and ORM classes depend on the SQLAlchemy base, Alembic config, and shared settings established in phase_1.
Kafka schemas reference domain model types from phase_2; Kafka admin and topic setup rely on shared config from phase_1.
WorkspaceManager and LocalGitOps reference workspace/checkpoint models from phase_2 and emit Kafka events using the producer from phase_3.
Temporal workflows and activities operate on domain models from phase_2 and emit/consume Kafka events via the infrastructure from phase_3.
CLI commands invoke workspace operations (phase_4), trigger Temporal workflows (phase_5), and publish Kafka events (phase_3). The CLI is the integration point for all three.
Machine worker consumes Kafka task assignments (phase_3), manages workspace artifacts (phase_4), and executes Temporal activities (phase_5).
AI worker consumes Kafka events (phase_3), reads workspace/git context (phase_4), and runs as a Temporal activity (phase_5). LangGraph tools reference git and artifact operations from phase_4.
Policy engine evaluates rules against domain model entities (phase_2) and emits POLICY_VIOLATION_DETECTED events via Kafka (phase_3).

REST API routers query ORM models (phase_2), the WebSocket server streams Kafka events (phase_3), and API endpoints expose Temporal workflow state (phase_5). Client code in phase_12 must see actual route paths and response shapes.
Observability consumer subscribes to Kafka topics (phase_3) and records metrics against domain model event types (phase_2).

React UI client code (api/client.ts, useWorkflowSocket.ts) calls REST endpoints and connects to the WebSocket server defined in phase_10. The client must know actual route paths, response shapes, and WebSocket protocol before being written.

Advanced UI panels (CheckpointViewer, HandoffPanel, AITraceViewer, ReplayControls) build on top of the store, hooks, and base components established in phase_12. They are integrated into App.tsx which was scaffolded in phase_12.

Test suite covers all backend components — unit tests for models, Kafka, workspace, git, DSL parser, and policy engine; integration tests for Kafka fanout, workflow lifecycle, and task handoff. All implementation phases must be complete before tests can be written against them.

Example workflow YAMLs and README document the full system end-to-end including the UI (phase_13). The docker-compose.yml update adds all application services, which requires knowing all service configurations established from phase_1 onward. phase_13 completion ensures the UI is fully described in documentation.

clarifying_questions.exploration_summary: FlowOS is a greenfield distributed orchestration platform described in a single comprehensive design document (`flowos_full_design_v2.md`, ~1,310 lines, 26 sections). There is no existing code — this is a pure design-to-implementation task. The system is described as a 'stateful collaboration OS' where humans, machines, and AI agents all act as first-class agents collaborating through durable, event-driven workflows. The architecture has 7 major layers: (1) Orchestrator (Temporal for durable workflow execution), (2) Event Bus (NATS or Kafka), (3) CLI Agent (Python or Go, runs on every node), (4) AI Reasoning (LangGraph + LangChain as pluggable modules inside task boundaries), (5) Execution (Kubernetes + Argo Workflows), (6) State/Metadata (Postgres + optional Neo4j + optional vector store), and (7) UI (React + React Flow + WebSockets). Every node maintains a local Git-backed workspace with a defined directory structure under `/flowos/workspaces/{workflow_id}/{task_id}/`. Eight domain entities are fully specified with JSON schemas: Workflow, Task, Agent, Event, Workspace, Checkpoint, Handoff, and ReasoningRun. A YAML-based workflow DSL is defined. The MVP is broken into 3 phases: Phase 1 (Temporal + event bus + CLI + Git workspace + checkpoint/revert + handoff), Phase 2 (machine workers + build/test + LangChain tools + artifact storage + UI), Phase 3 (LangGraph reasoning + policy engine + workflow DSL + branch lineage). Key open decisions include: event bus technology choice (NATS vs Kafka), CLI language (Python vs Go), MVP scope boundaries, testing strategy, and authentication/authorization design.

tech_stack.language: Python or Go (CLI agent — not yet decided)
tech_stack.framework: Temporal (durable orchestration) + NATS or Kafka (event bus)
key_libraries: Temporal SDK - durable workflow execution, signals, activities, timers
LangGraph - graph-based AI reasoning inside task boundaries
LangChain - LLM abstraction, RAG, tool integration
GitPython or native git - local workspace management per node
React Flow - workflow graph visualization in UI
tech_stack.database: Postgres (structured metadata) + S3-compatible object storage (artifacts/snapshots) + optional Neo4j (graph/lineage) + optional Qdrant/pgvector (vector store)
tech_stack.testing: Not defined in design document — needs to be established

questions.question: Which MVP phase should the implementation plan focus on first, and how strictly should scope be bounded?
questions.context: The design defines 3 MVP phases. Phase 1 alone (Temporal + event bus + CLI + Git workspace + checkpoint/revert + handoff) is already a substantial system. Without clear scope boundaries, the plan could sprawl across all 3 phases and never reach a working state.

options.label: Phase 1 only — strict MVP
options.description: Implement only Phase 1: Temporal orchestration, event bus, CLI agent, local Git workspace, checkpoint/revert, and basic handoff. No UI, no AI integration, no machine workers. Produces a working but minimal system.

options.label: Phase 1 + Phase 2 core
options.description: Implement Phase 1 fully plus the most critical Phase 2 items: machine execution workers, basic UI visualization, and artifact storage. Skips LangChain/LangGraph integration for now. Produces a usable system with visibility.

options.label: All 3 phases — full design
options.description: Plan and implement all 3 phases end-to-end: full orchestration, AI reasoning, policy engine, workflow DSL, branch lineage, and UI. Comprehensive but high-risk and long timeline.

questions.question: Which language should be used for the CLI agent and backend services?
questions.context: The design suggests Python or Go for the CLI agent. This decision affects the entire codebase — LangChain/LangGraph have first-class Python support, while Go offers better performance and distribution for CLI tools. The backend services (Temporal workers, event bus consumers) also need a language choice.

options.label: Python throughout
options.description: Use Python for CLI agent, Temporal workers, and backend services. Best ecosystem fit for LangChain/LangGraph AI integration. Easier to prototype but slower runtime and harder to distribute as a standalone CLI binary.

options.label: Go for CLI, Python for AI services
options.description: Use Go for the CLI agent and core backend services (fast, single binary distribution, strong Temporal Go SDK), and Python microservices for AI reasoning tasks (LangGraph/LangChain). Polyglot but clean separation of concerns.

options.label: Go throughout
options.description: Use Go for everything including AI integration layers. Requires wrapping LangChain/LangGraph via subprocess calls or HTTP APIs. Best performance and distribution but loses native AI framework integration.

questions.question: Which event bus technology should be used for the initial implementation?
questions.context: The design offers NATS (lightweight, low-latency), Kafka (enterprise-scale, replayable), or Redis Streams (small setups). This is a foundational infrastructure decision that is difficult to change later and affects event replay, durability, and operational complexity.

options.label: NATS JetStream
options.description: Use NATS with JetStream for persistence and replay. Simpler to operate than Kafka, lower latency, good for the MVP. Can be replaced with Kafka later if scale demands it. Best fit for Phase 1.

options.label: Apache Kafka
options.description: Use Kafka from the start. Better event replay semantics, stronger durability guarantees, and enterprise-grade. Higher operational complexity but avoids a future migration. Best if production scale is the immediate target.

options.label: Redis Streams (MVP only)
options.description: Use Redis Streams for the MVP to minimize infrastructure complexity, with a planned migration to NATS or Kafka before production. Fastest to get started but creates technical debt.

questions.question: What should the authentication and authorization model be for agents and users?
questions.context: The design mentions tokens or mTLS for agent authentication and role-based assignment control, but provides no specifics. This affects how human users log in, how machine agents are provisioned, how AI nodes are authorized, and how the policy engine enforces access controls.

options.label: JWT tokens + RBAC
options.description: Use JWT tokens for all agent types (human, machine, AI) with role-based access control enforced at the orchestrator and event bus level. Simple to implement, widely understood, good for MVP.

options.label: mTLS for machines + JWT for humans
options.description: Use mTLS certificates for machine and AI agents (stronger identity guarantees for automated nodes) and JWT/OAuth2 for human users. More secure but higher operational complexity for certificate management.

options.label: API keys (MVP) with auth upgrade later
options.description: Use simple API keys for the MVP to unblock development, with a planned upgrade to JWT/mTLS before production. Fastest path to a working system but defers security hardening.

questions.question: What testing strategy should be established for this system?
questions.context: The design document defines no testing strategy. FlowOS involves Temporal workflows, event bus integration, Git operations, AI framework calls, and a CLI — each requiring different testing approaches. Without a clear strategy, coverage will be inconsistent and the system will be fragile.

options.label: Unit + integration + Temporal replay tests
options.description: Unit tests for domain logic and CLI commands, integration tests for event bus and Git operations, and Temporal's built-in workflow replay testing for orchestration logic. Comprehensive and aligned with Temporal best practices.

options.label: Unit tests only for MVP
options.description: Focus on unit tests for the MVP to move fast, with integration and e2e tests added in Phase 2. Faster initial development but higher risk of integration bugs going undetected.

options.label: BDD/scenario-driven e2e tests
options.description: Define end-to-end scenarios (e.g., 'Design → Code → Build → Review' workflow) as the primary test suite, with unit tests only for critical utilities. Tests mirror real usage but are slower to write and run.

questions.question: How should the UI be prioritized and scoped in the implementation plan?
questions.context: The design specifies a rich UI with workflow graph visualization, live state, event timeline, diff viewer, replay controls, and AI trace viewer. This is substantial frontend work. The question is whether UI should be built in parallel with the backend or deferred until the core system is stable.

options.label: CLI-first, UI deferred to Phase 2
options.description: Build the complete CLI and backend in Phase 1 with no UI. Add the React + React Flow UI in Phase 2 once the backend APIs are stable. Reduces Phase 1 scope significantly and avoids building UI against unstable APIs.

options.label: Minimal read-only UI in Phase 1
options.description: Build a minimal read-only dashboard (workflow list, task status, event stream) in Phase 1 alongside the CLI. Full interactive UI (graph, diff viewer, replay) deferred to Phase 2. Provides visibility without full UI complexity.

options.label: Full UI built in parallel
options.description: Build the full UI in parallel with the backend from the start. Requires a stable API contract early and a dedicated frontend effort. Produces the most complete system but highest parallel complexity.

questions.question: How should the implementation be broken down and validated — monorepo or separate services?
questions.context: FlowOS has multiple distinct components: Temporal workers, CLI agent, event bus consumers, AI reasoning services, UI, and metadata API. These can be organized as a monorepo (single repo, multiple packages) or as separate repositories/services. This affects development velocity, deployment, and testing.

options.label: Monorepo with clear package boundaries
options.description: Single repository with packages for: core domain models, Temporal workflows/activities, CLI agent, event bus adapters, AI reasoning adapters, metadata API, and UI. Easier to develop and test together, single source of truth for interfaces.

options.label: Separate repos per service
options.description: Separate repositories for each major service (orchestrator, CLI, event consumers, AI service, UI). Better for independent deployment and team ownership but higher coordination overhead for a small team.

options.label: Monorepo with Docker Compose for local dev
options.description: Monorepo with Docker Compose for local development and testing of all services together. Each service is independently deployable but developed in a single repo. Best balance of development velocity and deployment flexibility.

completion_summary.headline: FlowOS — Distributed Human + Machine Orchestration Platform: Full Implementation Plan — 17 phases completed, 151 files created
